en.extract:
Costa's hummingbird (Calypte costae) is a species of hummingbird in the family Trochilidae. It breeds in the arid, semi-arid and Mediterranean climate zones of the Southwestern United States and northwestern Mexico, mainly in Southern California and the Baja California Peninsula. Like many hummingbirds in the region, it can be found on the Pacific coast, often visiting backyard hummingbird feeders and gardens with flowering plants, as well as in more wild ecosystems, such as the conifer and oak forests, chaparral, and high desert habitats found further inland. When not in the US, the species often winters in western Mexico.


Taxonomy
Costa's hummingbird was formally described by the French ornithologist Jules Bourcier in 1839, and given the binomial name Ornismya costae; Bourcier chose the specific epithet to honour French nobleman (and fellow ornithologist) Louis Marie Pantaleon Costa, Marquis de Beauregard (1806–1864). The type locality is Magdalena Bay, on the Pacific west coast, of the Mexican state of Baja California Sur. Costa's hummingbird is presently placed in the genus Calypte, introduced in 1856 by John Gould. The species is monotypic, with no subspecies being currently recognised.
Hybrids of C. costae and Anna's (C. anna), black-chinned (Archilochus alexandri), and broad-tailed hummingbirds (Selasphorus platycercus) are all known to have occurred.


Description
This species is small, as a mature adult grows to only 3–3.5 in (7.6–8.9 cm) in length, with a wingspan of 11 cm, and an average weight of 3.05 g for males and 3.22 g for females. The male has a mainly green back and flanks, a small black tail and wings, and patches of white below their gorgeted throat and tail. Its most distinguishing feature is its vibrant purple cap and throat with the throat feathers flaring out and back behind its head. The female is not as distinct as the male, having grayish-green above with a white underbelly.


Distribution and habitat
Costa's hummingbird is common in the arid brushy deserts and gardens of the Southwestern United States and the Baja California Peninsula of Mexico.


Behavior


Breeding

The male Costa's hummingbird's courtship display is a spirited series of swoops and arcing dives, using an angle to the sun to display its violet extended gorget to prospective mates. The male makes a high-speed dive pass or hover close to the female, perched on a nearby branch, and is accented by a high-pitched vibration that is produced by tail feathers. Separately, the male will perch and produce a high-pitched, scratch-like song.
The female constructs a small cup-shaped nest out of plant fibers, down, and at times spider silk, coated with lichen to hold it together. The nest is situated on a yucca stalk or tree limb. The female lays just two eggs, which are white in color, which she will incubate for 15 to 18 days before the young hatch. The young leave the nest after 20 to 23 days.


Diet
As common with hummingbird species, Costa's hummingbird feeds on flower nectar and small insects. While feeding in flight, a Costa's hummingbird may hover.


Torpor
As with other hummingbird species, the Costa's hummingbird can slow its metabolism during cold nights when it enters a hibernation-like state known as torpor, with its heart rate and respiration slowing substantially.


Conservation
The IUCN describes them as of least concern. The population is stable and there are no known threats. They are found in at least one protected area.


References


External links

"Costa's Hummingbird media". Internet Bird Collection.
Costa's Hummingbird photo gallery at VIREO (Drexel University)

'''Costa's hummingbird''' ('''''Calypte costae''''') is a [[species]] of [[hummingbird]] in the [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] Trochilidae.<ref name="cornell">{{cite web |title=Costa's hummingbird |url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Costas_Hummingbird/ |publisher=All About Birds, Cornell University |access-date=27 December 2023 |date=2023}}</ref> It breeds in the [[Aridoamerica|arid]], [[Semi-arid climate|semi-arid]] and [[Mediterranean climate|Mediterranean]] climate zones of the [[Southwestern United States]] and northwestern [[Mexico]], mainly in [[Southern California]] and the [[Baja California peninsula|Baja California Peninsula]]. Like many hummingbirds in the region, it can be found on the [[Pacific Ocean|Pacific]] coast, often visiting backyard [[Bird feeder|hummingbird feeders]] and gardens with [[Flowering plant|flowering plants]], as well as in more wild [[Ecosystem|ecosystems]], such as the [[conifer]] and [[oak]] forests, [[chaparral]], and high [[desert]] habitats found further inland. When not in the US, the species often winters in western Mexico.<ref name=cornell/>

==Taxonomy==
Costa's hummingbird was [[species description|formally described]] by the French [[Ornithology|ornithologist]] [[Jules Bourcier]] in 1839, and given the [[Binomial nomenclature|binomial name]] ''Ornismya costae'';<ref>{{cite journal | last=Bourcier | first=Jules | author-link=Jules Bourcier | year=1839 | title=Description de quelques espèces nouvelles d'oiseaux-mouches | language=French | journal=Revue Zoologique | volume=2 | pages=294–295 [294] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/2299310 }}</ref> Bourcier chose the [[Specific name (zoology)|specific epithet]] to honour French nobleman (and fellow ornithologist) [[Pantaléon Costa de Beauregard|Louis Marie Pantaleon Costa]], [[Marquess|Marquis]] de Beauregard (1806–1864).<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=120 }}</ref> The [[type locality (biology)|type locality]] is [[Magdalena Bay]], on the [[Pacific Ocean|Pacific]] west coast, of the [[List of states of Mexico|Mexican state]] of [[Baja California Sur]].<ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1945 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=5 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | pages=136–137 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14480147 }}</ref> Costa's hummingbird is presently placed in the genus ''Calypte'', introduced in 1856 by [[John Gould]].<ref>{{ cite book | last=Gould | first=John | author-link=John Gould | year=1856 | title=A Monograph of the Trochilidae, or Family of Humming-Birds | volume=4 | location=London | publisher=self | at=Plates 134, 135, 136 and text (Part 11, Plates 5, 6 and 7) | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/35246269 }} The 5 volumes were issued in 25 parts between 1849 and 1861. Title pages of all volumes bear the date of 1861.</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date=January 2021 | title=Hummingbirds | work=IOC World Bird List Version 11.1 | url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/hummingbirds/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=1 March 2021 }}</ref> The species is [[monotypic]], with no [[subspecies]] being currently recognised.<ref name=ioc/>

[[Hybrid (biology)|Hybrids]] of ''C. costae'' and [[Anna's hummingbird|Anna's]] (''C. anna''), [[black-chinned hummingbird|black-chinned]] (''Archilochus alexandri''), and [[broad-tailed hummingbird|broad-tailed hummingbirds]] (''Selasphorus platycercus'') are all known to have occurred.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Huey, Laurence M. |year=1944|title=A hybrid Costa's x Broad-tailed hummingbird|journal=[[Auk (journal)|The Auk]]|volume=61|issue=4|pages=636–637|url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v061n04/p0636-p0637.pdf|doi=10.2307/4080185|jstor=4080185}}</ref>

==Description==
This species is small, as a mature adult grows to only {{convert|3|-|3.5|in|cm|abbr=on}} in length, with a wingspan of 11 cm,<ref>{{Cite web|last=Oiseaux.net|title=Colibri de Costa - Calypte costae - Costa's Hummingbird|url=https://www.oiseaux.net/birds/costa.s.hummingbird.html|access-date=2020-09-26|website=www.oiseaux.net|language=en}}</ref> and an average weight of 3.05 g for males and 3.22 g for females.<ref name=cornell/><ref name="h.net">{{Cite web|title=Costa's hummingbird|url=http://www.hummingbirds.net/costas.html|access-date=2020-09-26|website=www.hummingbirds.net}}</ref> The male has a mainly green back and flanks, a small black tail and wings, and patches of white below their [[gorget (birds)|gorget]]ed throat and tail. Its most distinguishing feature is its vibrant purple cap and throat with the throat feathers flaring out and back behind its head.<ref name=cornell/> The female is not as distinct as the male, having grayish-green above with a white underbelly.<ref name=cornell/>

==Distribution and habitat==
Costa's hummingbird is common in the arid brushy deserts and gardens of the [[Southwestern United States]] and the [[Baja California Peninsula]] of [[Mexico]].<ref name=cornell/>

==Behavior==
===Breeding===
[[File:Unidentified hummingbird by Pete Gregoire (9363245042).jpg|thumb|Male Costa's hummingbird]]
The male Costa's hummingbird's courtship display is a spirited series of swoops and arcing dives, using an angle to the sun to display its violet extended<!--photo needed--> [[Gorget (bird)|gorget]] to prospective mates.<ref>{{cite web |title=No CGI here: The Costa's hummingbird woos mates with this freaky face |url=https://www.earthtouchnews.com/wtf/wtf/no-cgi-here-the-costas-hummingbird-woos-mates-with-this-freaky-face/ |website=Earth Touch News Network |language=en |date=2014}}</ref> The male makes a high-speed dive pass or [[Hover (behaviour)|hover]] close to the female, perched on a nearby branch, and is accented by a high-pitched vibration that is produced by tail feathers. Separately, the male will perch and produce a high-pitched, scratch-like song.<ref name=cornell/>

The female constructs a small cup-shaped nest out of plant fibers, down, and at times [[spider silk]], coated with [[lichen]] to hold it together.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Woods | first= Robert S. | title=The development of young Costa hummingbirds | journal=The Condor |issue=6|volume=24|pages=189–193|year=1922|doi=10.2307/1362578 | jstor= 1362578 | url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/condor/v024n06/p0189-p0193.pdf }}</ref> The nest is situated on a [[yucca]] stalk or tree limb. The female lays just two eggs, which are white in color, which she will incubate for 15 to 18 days before the young hatch. The young leave the nest after 20 to 23 days.

===Diet===
As common with hummingbird species, Costa's hummingbird feeds on flower [[nectar]] and small insects. While feeding in flight, a Costa's hummingbird may hover.
<gallery>
Kolibri-Sonora-Desert-USA.jpg|Female hovering
Calypte costae-male hovering nectaring.jpg|Male hovering
</gallery>

===Torpor===
As with other hummingbird species, the Costa's hummingbird can slow its [[metabolism]] during cold nights when it enters a hibernation-like state known as [[torpor]], with its heart rate and respiration slowing substantially.<ref name=cornell/>

==Conservation==
The IUCN describes them as of least concern. The population is stable and there are no known threats. They are found in at least one protected area.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/en|title=The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species|website=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species}}</ref>

zh.extract:
丽羽蜂鸟（学名：Calypte costae），又名科氏蜂鸟或紫喉蜂鳥，是雨燕目蜂鸟科的一种鸟类。
丽羽蜂鸟体重约3.09克，翼长约44.7毫米，嘴峰长约19.2毫米，喙宽度约1.6毫米，喙厚度约1.6毫米，跗蹠长约4.4毫米，尾长约23.6毫米。丽羽蜂鸟是候鸟，其栖息在半开放的灌木丛中，食性为草食性，主要食物来源是植物的花蜜。
丽羽蜂鸟分布于美国西南部至下加利福尼亚南部和墨西哥西北部。该物种是单型物种，没有亚种分化。
